results.possibleIdentity: Tony Martin (1937–2021), also known as Anthony Martin, was an American painter and pioneering multimedia artist influential in the development of light art and interactive installations during the late 1960s and 1970s. Born in Knoxville, Tennessee, he was the son of artist David Stone Martin and began his career in the early 1960s in San Francisco and Chicago. He became the first Visual Director of the San Francisco Tape Music Center in 1963, where he collaborated with avant-garde composers such as Pauline Oliveros, Morton Subotnick, Terry Riley, and Ramon Sender to create 'light-music' performances. Martin is widely recognized for inventing the psychedelic liquid light shows used by Bill Graham at the Fillmore West for bands like the Grateful Dead and Jefferson Airplane. In the late 1960s, he moved to New York and became deeply involved with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), most notably designing the complex light system for the E.A.T. Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. His work often focused on viewer-participation and sensory environments, exemplified by his 1967 breakthrough sculpture 'The Well' and his interactive 'Game Room' (1968) exhibited at the Howard Wise Gallery. Throughout his six-decade career, he maintained a dual practice of technological media and traditional oil painting, often cross-fertilizing the two disciplines.

segment.text: Born in Knoxville, Tennessee, he was the son of artist David Stone Martin and began his career in the early 1960s in San Francisco and Chicago.

He became the first Visual Director of the San Francisco Tape Music Center in 1963, where he collaborated with avant-garde composers such as Pauline Oliveros, Morton Subotnick, Terry Riley, and Ramon Sender to create 'light-music' performances.
Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan.
